q: In Power Query Editor, you open a new table and want to understand its data quality before transforming it. Which built-in profiling features do you enable, and what does each tell you?
a: Power Query Editor offers three column profiling views, all toggled from the View ribbon. Column quality shows the percentage of valid, error, and empty values for each column—letting you spot bad rows at a glance. Column distribution renders a bar chart of distinct vs. unique value counts, helping identify low-cardinality columns (candidates for a category data type) and high-cardinality ones (candidates for keys). Column profile (available for a selected column) provides count, distinct, unique, min, max, average, standard deviation, and value distribution histogram—essential for deciding whether numeric ranges are sensible before loading. By default profiling is based on the top 1,000 rows; switch it to 'entire data set' for accuracy on large tables. Together these three views replace manual SQL DESCRIBE or PROFILING queries and give data quality evidence before you write a single M transformation step.
fj: Column distribution shows you the min, max, and average for a selected column.
fs: Column distribution shows distinct and unique value counts with a bar chart—it does not compute statistical summaries. The statistical summaries (min, max, average, standard deviation) are in the Column profile panel, which appears at the bottom of the editor when you select a single column.

q: A date column imported from a CSV is detected as 'text' type in Power Query. What is the correct process to set it to Date, and why must data type assignment happen before downstream transformation steps?
a: Right-click the column header (or use the Transform ribbon > Data Type) and select Date. Power Query inserts a 'Changed Type' step in the Applied Steps pane. The assignment should happen as early as possible—ideally immediately after the Source step—because subsequent steps that rely on the column (date arithmetic, filters, relationship keys) all operate on the type inferred at creation time. If you change the type after a filter, the filter was applied to the text representation, not the date value, so comparisons like [OrderDate] > #date(2024,1,1) would have failed silently or errored. Additionally, importing data into the model with the correct type avoids Power BI automatically inferring a Text column in the field list, which prevents time-intelligence functions from recognising the column as a date. Always validate the detected locale for date strings (e.g., MM/DD/YYYY vs DD/MM/YYYY) using Column Tools > Data Category or the type-with-locale option to prevent silent misparses.
fj: You should change the data type in the Report view by updating the column's Data Type property in the Fields pane.
fs: The Fields pane in Report view only formats how values are displayed—it does not change the underlying storage type. Data type conversion that affects query folding, column storage, and downstream M steps must happen in Power Query Editor before the data is loaded into the model.

code.label: M: Changed Type step inserted by Power Query

q: A Power Query table has a column with null values and a column with inconsistent capitalisation (e.g., 'new york', 'New York', 'NEW YORK'). Walk through how you resolve both issues in the Query Editor.
a: For nulls: select the column, go to Transform > Replace Values, set 'Value to Find' to null (leave it blank in the dialog) and enter a sensible default, OR use Transform > Fill Down/Fill Up if nulls represent missing carry-forward values. For nulls that indicate genuinely unknown data, replacing with 'Unknown' preserves row count without distorting aggregations. For inconsistent capitalisation: select the column, go to Transform > Format > Capitalize Each Word (for proper-noun columns like city names) or UPPERCASE/lowercase as appropriate. This inserts a Text.Proper, Text.Upper, or Text.Lower M step. After both fixes, re-check Column Quality to confirm zero errors and zero empty values. If the null-filling pattern is conditional (e.g., only fill null when another column equals 'Active'), use Add Column > Conditional Column or the Table.AddColumn M formula to create a derived corrected column rather than mutating the source column.
fj: You should use a DAX ISBLANK() measure to handle nulls at query time.
fs: DAX ISBLANK() runs at report query time against the loaded model—it does not clean the data before it is loaded. Null handling must be done in Power Query so that null rows are not loaded into the model in the first place, which saves storage and prevents aggregation errors.

code.label: M: Replace null and fix capitalisation
lines: // Step 1 - replace nulls in Region column

q: You receive a sales spreadsheet where each month is a separate column (Jan, Feb, Mar … Dec). Your model needs a single Date and Amount column. How do you pivot or unpivot this in Power Query?
a: Select all twelve month columns, then go to Transform > Unpivot Columns (or 'Unpivot Other Columns' if you first select the non-month columns). Power Query generates an Attribute column containing the month names and a Value column containing the sales figures. Rename Attribute to 'Month' and Value to 'Amount'. This wide-to-tall transformation is the correct prep for time-intelligence—tall format lets a Date table join on the Month column, enabling SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR and YTD measures. To go the other direction (tall to wide), select the column whose distinct values should become column headers, choose Transform > Pivot Column, and select the value column to aggregate. The exam tests the distinction: Pivot turns rows into columns; Unpivot turns columns into rows. 'Transpose' swaps the entire table's rows and columns, including headers, and is rarely used in production pipelines.
fj: Transpose is the correct operation to convert a wide monthly table to a tall format.
fs: Transpose flips the entire table—rows become columns and columns become rows, including headers—which produces a table that still has months as the first column rather than as a single Attribute field. Unpivot is the correct operation to normalise wide monthly columns into a tall Date/Amount structure.

q: What is the difference between a Reference query and a Duplicate query in Power Query, and when should you choose each?
a: A Duplicate query copies all Applied Steps from the original into the new query—each query evaluates independently from the source. Use Duplicate when you need a completely separate transformation chain that starts from scratch alongside the original, or when you want a sandbox copy to experiment in without affecting the live query. A Reference query has only one step: its source is the original query's output. Any changes made upstream in the original query automatically flow through to the reference. Use Reference when multiple queries need the same cleaned base table (e.g., a filtered subset or a different join)—it avoids repeating transformation logic and ensures changes propagate consistently. The key performance implication: if the original query is expensive, referencing it means the engine can cache or fold the shared steps once; duplicating means the source is queried twice. For a shared staging query that five downstream queries depend on, Reference is the correct choice.
fj: Reference queries run faster than duplicate queries because they skip query folding.
fs: Query folding is determined by whether Power Query can translate M steps back to native source query language—it has no direct relationship to whether a query is a reference or a duplicate. The performance advantage of Reference is avoiding redundant re-evaluation of shared transformation steps, not bypassing query folding.

q: You need to combine data from a Sales table and a Returns table that share the same schema, and also join a Customers dimension. Which Power Query operations do you use, and what is the difference between them?
a: Use Append Queries to stack the Sales and Returns tables vertically—they share the same columns, so rows from Returns are added beneath rows from Sales, producing a single combined table. Use Merge Queries (the Power Query equivalent of a JOIN) to bring in Customers. You select the join key column(s) in both tables and choose a join kind: Left Outer returns all Sales rows and matching Customer data; Inner returns only matched rows; Full Outer returns all rows from both sides; Left/Right Anti return unmatched rows only. After merging, expand the Customer nested table column to surface the attributes you need. Append is analogous to SQL UNION ALL; Merge is analogous to SQL JOIN. A common exam trap is confusing Append with Merge—Append is always vertical (adds rows), Merge is always horizontal (adds columns).
fj: You should use Union in Power Query to stack the Sales and Returns tables.
fs: Power Query does not have a Union operation—Union is SQL terminology. The equivalent in Power Query is Append Queries, which stacks tables vertically. Confusing the two suggests misreading the UI; the correct menu item is Home > Append Queries.

q: When refreshing a Power BI report, you see a 'Data source error: credentials are required' message. How do you resolve data import errors related to credentials and privacy levels?
a: Navigate to File > Options and Settings > Data Source Settings in Power BI Desktop. Find the problematic data source and click Edit Permissions. Re-enter or update the credentials (Windows, Basic, OAuth, or Key depending on the connector). If the error appears in the Power BI service after publishing, go to the semantic model's Settings > Data Source Credentials and re-authenticate there—Desktop and Service credentials are stored separately. Privacy levels (None, Public, Organizational, Private) control whether Power Query can combine data from multiple sources. If you see 'Formula.Firewall: Query references other queries or steps and so it may not directly access a data source', set the privacy level for each source appropriately in Data Source Settings > Edit Permissions > Privacy Level, or configure a single privacy level in File > Options > Privacy > Always ignore privacy level settings (acceptable only in development). Incorrect privacy levels block query folding across sources rather than failing with a credentials error, so distinguish the two error classes.
fj: You can fix credential errors by setting the privacy level to 'None' in the report's Data Model settings.
fs: Privacy levels are configured per data source in Data Source Settings, not in Data Model settings. Setting privacy to None means no privacy is applied and data can be sent to external sources—this is different from fixing authentication. Credential errors require re-entering valid authentication tokens or passwords for the specific connector.

q: A Power Query step produces an error for rows where a division-by-zero occurs. How do you gracefully handle per-row errors in M without removing valid rows?
a: Wrap the expression in a try...otherwise construct. This evaluates the expression and, if it produces an error, returns the fallback value instead of propagating the error row. Use Table.TransformColumns to apply this pattern to a whole column at once. Alternatively, use Add Column > Custom Column with the same try...otherwise syntax to produce a new column that captures either the result or a safe default. For numeric division errors specifically, it is also valid to guard with an if denominator = 0 then null else numerator / denominator pattern, which prevents the error from occurring rather than catching it afterwards. The 'Remove Errors' option in Power Query removes entire rows where any error exists—only use this when error rows are genuinely invalid rather than when you need a fallback value.
fj: Use Remove Errors from the Home ribbon to handle rows with division-by-zero issues.
fs: Remove Errors deletes the affected rows entirely from the result set. If the row contains valid data in other columns (e.g., a sale with a zero-quantity denominator), removing it loses valid business data. The correct approach is to catch the error with try...otherwise and substitute a safe default value.

q: Explain when you would add a custom column versus creating a conditional column in Power Query, and provide an example of each.
a: Use Add Column > Custom Column when the logic requires a full M expression—calculations, text concatenation, calling functions, or multi-condition formulas with else-if chains. Custom columns accept any valid M expression and can reference other columns using [ColumnName] syntax. Use Add Column > Conditional Column when the logic is a simple if-then-else based on comparing a single column to a fixed value—Power Query provides a GUI wizard that emits an if/then/else M expression without requiring you to write code. A conditional column is essentially a restricted subset of what a custom column can do. Example custom column: [Quantity] * [UnitPrice] * (1 - [DiscountRate]). Example conditional: if [Region] = 'West' then 'Western' else 'Other'. The exam may also test that you can recognise the generated M syntax for both, since Applied Steps show the raw M for review.
fj: Conditional columns support complex nested expressions like string manipulation and arithmetic formulas.
fs: The Conditional Column dialog is a GUI wizard for simple single-column if/then/else comparisons with fixed values. It cannot perform arithmetic, string functions, or multi-column logic. Complex logic requires a Custom Column with a full M expression.

code.label: M: Custom column vs Conditional column

q: How do you Group By rows in Power Query to compute a total per category, and what aggregation options are available?
a: Select Transform > Group By (or Home > Group By). In the dialog choose the column(s) to group by and add one or more aggregation operations. Available aggregations include Sum, Average, Median, Min, Max, Count Rows, Count Distinct Rows, and All Rows (which returns the raw sub-table for each group, useful for custom aggregations). Click Advanced to define multiple grouping columns and multiple aggregations at once. Group By emits a Table.Group M step. A common use case is computing total sales per Region per Year before joining a target table. The 'Count Rows' aggregation counts all rows in the group regardless of null; 'Count Distinct Rows' on a specific column counts unique non-null values—these behave differently and choosing the wrong one is a common exam trap.
fj: Group By in Power Query only supports Sum and Count aggregations.
fs: Power Query's Group By dialog supports Sum, Average, Median, Min, Max, Count Rows, Count Distinct Rows, and the special All Rows option. It is not limited to two aggregations.

code.label: M: Group By with multiple aggregations

q: A REST API returns a JSON array with nested objects. How do you convert this semi-structured payload into a flat relational table in Power Query?
a: When Power Query connects to a JSON or web source, it initially returns a List or Record type. Click 'To Table' (from the List Tools ribbon or the Transform > Convert To Table option) to convert the list of records into a Table. Each row becomes a record; columns are represented as Record type initially. Click the expand icon (double-headed arrow) at the top of the Record column to expand nested fields into individual columns—uncheck any fields you don't need. For deeply nested arrays within a record (e.g., an order with a line_items array), repeat the expand step on the nested list column: it will produce one row per nested item, effectively joining the parent fields to each child row (similar to an UNNEST or LATERAL JOIN). Remove any [List] or [Record] typed columns that were not expanded. This technique also applies to XML sources where Power Query surfaces a Document/Table record structure.
fj: You should parse JSON using a custom M function that iterates each row with a for-each loop.
fs: Power Query's built-in expand operations (To Table, Expand Record, Expand List) handle JSON nesting declaratively without writing iteration loops. Writing custom recursive M functions for standard JSON expansion is unnecessary and harder to maintain.

q: A Power BI report needs to refresh against an on-premises SQL Server but the data source credentials are failing in the service. Walk through how to update data source credentials and set the correct privacy level after publishing.
a: In the Power BI service, navigate to the semantic model > Settings > Data Source Credentials. Each data source in the model is listed; click 'Edit credentials' next to the SQL Server entry. Select the authentication method (Windows or Basic), enter credentials, and save. If an on-premises gateway is required (which it is for on-premises SQL Server), the gateway must be configured first and the data source registered within that gateway—credentials entered in Desktop are not transferred to the service. For privacy levels, each data source has a Privacy Level setting (None, Public, Organizational, Private) accessible from the same edit credentials dialog. Set Organizational for internal sources that should share data only within the tenant, and Private for highly sensitive sources that must not be combined with other sources without explicit approval. Mismatched privacy levels cause Formula.Firewall errors during refresh if sources with different levels are being combined in a query.
fj: Credentials set in Power BI Desktop are automatically synced to the Power BI service when you publish.
fs: Desktop credentials are stored locally and are never transmitted to the Power BI service during publish. Credentials must be separately re-entered in the service's Data Source Credentials settings, or configured within the gateway for on-premises sources. This is a security design: publishing a report should not expose stored credentials.

q: Why does every Power BI model need a proper date table, and what are the requirements for marking a table as a date table?
a: DAX time-intelligence functions (SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR, TOTALYTD, DATESINPERIOD, etc.) require a contiguous, complete date table to evaluate period comparisons correctly. Without one, Power BI auto-generates a hidden date table for each date column, which is inefficient, inconsistent, and breaks when using DirectQuery mode. A compliant date table must: (1) contain a column of data type Date or DateTime with no duplicates and no blanks; (2) cover complete years—every date from January 1 of the earliest year to December 31 of the latest year, with no gaps; (3) be marked as a date table via Table Tools > Mark as Date Table. Marking it applies implicit REMOVEFILTERS on that date column inside time-intelligence functions, which is what makes year-to-date and period-over-period calculations work correctly. Common methods to create the table: CALENDARAUTO() DAX function, a Power Query M List.Dates expression, or importing a pre-built DimDate from a data warehouse.
fj: You only need to mark a table as a date table if it contains more than one year of data.
fs: The Mark as Date Table requirement is not based on the span of data. You must mark the table if you want DAX time-intelligence functions to work correctly—the marking instructs the engine to use REMOVEFILTERS on the date column. Without this, functions like TOTALYTD may double-count filtered data.

code.label: DAX: Create date table with CALENDAR

q: A Power BI model is slow to refresh and the file size is unexpectedly large. You suspect unused columns and rows are inflating it. How do you identify and remove them?
a: Start in Power Query Editor: use View > Column Quality and Column Profile to identify columns where 100% of values are the same (no useful variance) or where the column is never used in relationships, visuals, or DAX. Unnecessary columns to remove include technical audit fields from source systems (row version, ETL timestamps), columns with all-null values, and high-cardinality text columns used only for debug purposes (e.g., raw JSON blobs). For rows, apply source-level filter steps early in the query so unnecessary data is excluded before loading—this enables query folding, which pushes the filter to the source system. In the model itself, use the Model view to check if any table has no active relationships and no measures referencing it. For the data model layer, right-click tables in Model view to confirm import sizes. Use DAX Studio (external tool) or the built-in DAX query view to run a COLUMN_STATISTICS or INFO.STORAGETABLECOLUMNS query to see per-column size. Remove unused columns directly in Power Query (Remove Columns step) or by hiding them in the model (hiding does not reduce size—deletion does).
fj: Hiding columns in the Fields pane reduces the model file size.
fs: Hiding a column only removes it from the report canvas—it remains in the model and occupies the same storage. To reduce model size, columns must be deleted in Power Query Editor using Remove Columns, or filtered out before load.

q: How do you use the Performance Analyzer and DAX query view to identify which measures or visuals are causing slow report load times?
a: In Power BI Desktop, open the View ribbon and enable Performance Analyzer. Click Start Recording, then interact with the report or click Refresh Visuals. Each visual logs three time components: DAX query (time for Power BI to send the query and receive results from the engine), Visual display (time for the visual to render), and Other (overhead). Focus on visuals with the highest DAX query time. Click Copy Query next to a slow visual to extract the DAX query. Paste it into the DAX query view (introduced in Power BI Desktop 2023+) or into DAX Studio via External Tools. In DAX query view, click Run to see results and execution details inline. In DAX Studio, enable Server Timings to see storage engine (SE) vs. formula engine (FE) breakdown—high FE time indicates complex DAX iteration; high SE scan time indicates too much data being scanned. Common fixes: replace FILTER(ALL(Table), ...) with CALCULATETABLE patterns, ensure relationships are active and use integer keys, reduce the number of measures on a single visual, and avoid using many-to-many relationships where one-to-many suffices.
fj: The Performance Analyzer shows total CPU usage across all visuals to help identify the slowest machine.
fs: Performance Analyzer shows per-visual timings broken into DAX query, visual display, and other—it does not measure CPU or machine performance. It is a report-layer diagnostic tool, not a system monitor.

code.label: DAX: Optimise FILTER to CALCULATETABLE
lines: -- Slow: FILTER iterates the full table
-- Fast: CALCULATETABLE pushes filter to storage engine

q: Your model has a single Date table but it needs to be related to both an OrderDate and a ShipDate column in the Sales table. How do you implement role-playing dimensions?
a: Power BI does not support two active relationships between the same pair of tables. The standard pattern for role-playing dimensions is to create additional views or copies of the date table (one per role), each with a descriptive name: DimOrderDate, DimShipDate. In Power Query, create two reference queries off the base date table and rename them. Define an active relationship from DimOrderDate[Date] to Sales[OrderDate] and an active relationship from DimShipDate[Date] to Sales[ShipDate]. Each dimension table is independent in the model, so time-intelligence measures written against DimOrderDate work on OrderDate, while measures against DimShipDate work on ShipDate. Alternatively, use a single DimDate with inactive relationships and the USERELATIONSHIP() DAX function inside a CALCULATE to activate the desired relationship at measure evaluation time—but this approach requires separate measures for each role and is harder to maintain in large models.
fj: You can define two active relationships between DimDate and Sales by setting one relationship's cardinality to many-to-many.
fs: Power BI enforces that only one active relationship can exist between any two tables, regardless of cardinality. Changing to many-to-many does not remove this constraint and would also introduce incorrect aggregation behaviour. Role-playing dimensions require either separate copies of the dimension table or USERELATIONSHIP() within measures.

q: What is a semi-additive measure, and how do you write one in DAX for a bank account closing balance scenario?
a: A semi-additive measure aggregates correctly across some dimensions (e.g., account, region) but not across time—summing account balances over months gives a meaningless total. The correct pattern for closing balance is to take the last known value for each time period. Use LASTDATE to return the last date in the current filter context and wrap it in CALCULATE to evaluate the balance expression on that single date. For sparse data (where not every day has a balance entry), LASTNONBLANK is more robust: it returns the last date in the date table for which the expression evaluates to a non-blank value. LASTDATE is faster but only correct when data is present on every date or at least on every period-end date. This pattern applies to inventory on-hand, headcount, account balances—any metric that represents a snapshot state rather than an additive flow.
fj: You can sum balances across months using SUM(Balances[Amount]) inside a time-intelligence function like TOTALYTD.
fs: TOTALYTD accumulates values across the year by summing—it is designed for additive measures like revenue. Summing closing balances across months double-counts, because January's closing balance and February's closing balance both include the same underlying funds. Semi-additive measures require LASTDATE or LASTNONBLANK, not a sum accumulation.

q: What table and column properties should you configure in Power BI Desktop to improve the report authoring experience, and why?
a: Key properties to configure: (1) Hidden — hide technical key columns, intermediate staging columns, and columns already embedded in measures so the Fields pane stays uncluttered. (2) Display Folder — group related measures and columns into folders (e.g., 'KPIs', 'Date Intelligence') for faster navigation. (3) Default summarization — set columns that should never be summed (e.g., Year, CustomerID) to 'Don't Summarize' to prevent accidental SUM when dragging to a visual. (4) Data Category — tag columns as City, Country, Postal Code, URL, or Image URL so Power BI renders maps and clickable images correctly. (5) Format string — set currency, percentage, or date formatting at the model level so all visuals inherit consistent formatting without per-visual manual adjustment. (6) Synonyms — add alternate names (via the Synonyms property in Q&A setup) so natural-language queries resolve correctly. These are all set in the Model view Properties pane.
fj: You must set default summarization in DAX measures because column-level summarization settings are ignored at runtime.
fs: Default summarization is a column-level property in the model, not a DAX setting. When a user drags a column directly to a visual without using a measure, Power BI applies the column's default summarization. Setting it to 'Don't Summarize' prevents inappropriate auto-aggregation of ID or ordinal columns.

q: What DAX statistical functions are available for use in measures, and when would you use MEDIANX over AVERAGEX?
a: DAX provides several statistical functions: AVERAGE/AVERAGEX (arithmetic mean), MEDIAN/MEDIANX (middle value), MIN/MINX, MAX/MAXX, STDEV.P/STDEV.S (population and sample standard deviation), VAR.P/VAR.S (variance), PERCENTILE.INC, and GEOMEAN. MEDIANX is preferred over AVERAGEX when the distribution is skewed or contains outliers. For example, median household income is more representative than mean because a few very high earners pull the mean upward. MEDIANX takes an iterator pattern (table, expression) like SUMX, evaluating the expression for each row and computing the median of results. AVERAGEX uses the same pattern but computes the mean. Use MEDIANX in scenarios like median order value, median delivery time, or median NPS score where outliers would distort AVERAGEX.
fj: DAX does not support median calculations; you must compute it using a custom RANKX-based formula.
fs: DAX natively includes MEDIAN (column-level) and MEDIANX (iterator pattern) functions, both of which compute the median directly without requiring RANKX workarounds. RANKX-based percentile estimates were a workaround before MEDIAN was added to DAX.

q: How do Quick Measures work in Power BI Desktop, and when are they useful for non-DAX users?
a: Quick Measures (right-click a field > New Quick Measure, or from the Modeling ribbon) present a guided dialog where you select a calculation type from a predefined list and drag fields into parameter slots. Power BI generates the full DAX measure for you and places it in the model—you can inspect and edit the generated DAX afterward. Supported templates include: running totals, YTD/QTD/MTD aggregations, moving averages, period-over-period change, percentage of grand total, weighted average, and filtered values. Quick Measures are useful for business users or report authors who need common patterns without memorising DAX syntax. The limitation is that templates are finite—complex custom logic or multi-step calculations still require hand-written DAX. After creation, review the generated formula: Quick Measures sometimes produce more complex DAX than a hand-written equivalent, which can affect readability and performance.
fj: Quick Measures generate Python scripts that are evaluated at refresh time.
fs: Quick Measures generate DAX expressions, not Python. The generated code is stored as a standard DAX measure inside the model and evaluated by the Analysis Services engine at query time, with no Python dependency.

q: What is the Copilot narrative visual in Power BI, and how do you add and configure it on a report page?
a: The Copilot narrative visual is an AI-generated text visual that summarizes data on the report page in natural language. It is available in the Visualizations pane as the 'Smart Narrative' or narrative icon; selecting the Copilot option within the dialog uses the generative AI version powered by Azure OpenAI. To add it: open the Visualizations pane, click the narrative icon, then in the 'Choose a narrative type' dialog select 'Copilot (preview)'. Select the scope—individual visuals, a page, or the entire report—and click Create. The narrative updates dynamically as users apply slicers or filters: when a user filters to a specific region, the narrative re-generates to describe that region's data. You can guide the narrative using custom prompts in the 'Curate the narrative' pane. Requirements: your Power BI tenant admin must have enabled Copilot in Fabric admin settings, and the workspace must be on an F64 or higher Fabric capacity (or equivalent Premium capacity P1+).
fj: The Copilot narrative visual is a static text box that an admin pre-writes and publishes with the report.
fs: The narrative visual is AI-generated and dynamically updates based on the data context and active filters at view time. It is not a pre-written static text box. The content is generated at render time by the Copilot AI model against the current data slice.

q: How do you use Copilot to create a new full report page in Power BI, and what does the suggested page workflow look like?
a: In Power BI Desktop or the Power BI service, open the Copilot pane (View > Copilot or the sparkle icon in the ribbon). In the Copilot chat interface, type a natural-language prompt describing the page you want, for example: 'Create a page that shows monthly revenue trend by region with a comparison to last year.' Copilot analyzes the semantic model's fields, measures, and relationships, then generates a report page with suggested visuals, including field placements and chart types. A preview appears in the Copilot pane; click Apply to add it to the report canvas. The page can be edited afterward like any manually created page. Copilot can also suggest report pages from the 'Create a report page' option, which proposes several page designs based on the model's metadata. Requirements are the same as for the narrative visual: Copilot must be enabled by the tenant admin and the workspace must be on a Fabric or Premium capacity that supports Copilot.
fj: Copilot creates new report pages by running a scheduled Python script that calls the Power BI REST API.
fs: Copilot creates report pages interactively through a natural-language prompt interface inside Power BI Desktop or the service. It does not use Python scripts or the REST API—it is an in-product AI assistant backed by a large language model with access to the model's schema.

q: What are bookmarks in Power BI, and how do you use them to create a saved view and wire it to a button for navigation?
a: A bookmark captures the complete state of a report page at a moment in time: filter context, slicer selections, visual visibility, scroll position, and spotlight state. To create one: View > Bookmarks pane > Add. Name it descriptively (e.g., 'West Region – Q4'). You can update a bookmark by right-clicking it and choosing Update. To wire it to a button: insert a button (Insert > Buttons > Blank or a preset), enable the Action toggle in the Format pane, set Type to Bookmark, and select the saved bookmark from the Bookmark dropdown. When a reader clicks the button, the report snaps to that bookmarked state. Bookmarks have two capture options: Data (filter/slicer state) and Display (visibility of visuals). You can deselect 'Data' to create a display-only bookmark that just shows/hides visuals without affecting filters—useful for show/hide toggle panels. Bookmark groups let you create mutually exclusive view states, like tab-style navigation.
fj: Bookmarks only capture slicer selections; they cannot store which visuals are visible or hidden.
fs: Bookmarks capture both Data state (slicers and filters) and Display state (visual visibility, spotlight, scroll position). Deselecting 'Data' in the bookmark options creates a display-only bookmark, which is specifically used for show/hide interactions. Limiting bookmarks to slicers alone is incorrect.

q: Explain how to set up drillthrough navigation in Power BI so that clicking a product in a summary chart jumps to a Product Detail page filtered to that product.
a: Create the destination detail page first. In the Visualizations pane's Filters section, drag the field that should drive the drillthrough (e.g., Product[ProductName]) into the 'Add drillthrough filters here' well. Power BI automatically adds a Back button to the page. On the source page, right-clicking any visual element that contains the drillthrough field will show a 'Drill through > Product Detail' menu option. For a button-based drillthrough: insert a button on the source page, toggle Action = On, set Type = Drill through, set Destination = 'Product Detail'. Use conditional formatting on the button's Enabled state to show it only when a single product context is active. Key setting: 'Keep all filters' toggle—when on, all report-level filters carry over to the detail page in addition to the drillthrough context; when off, only the drillthrough field filter is applied. Cross-report drillthrough is also supported: in the destination report's page settings, enable 'Allow use as drillthrough target' and choose the source field.
fj: Drillthrough requires you to embed the detail visuals inside a hidden panel on the same report page using bookmarks.
fs: Drillthrough creates a separate destination page with its own canvas—not a hidden panel. The user navigates to that page with context preserved. The hidden-panel-plus-bookmark pattern is a different technique for expanding detail within the same page view and is not drillthrough.

q: How do you format and configure a bar chart visual in Power BI to meet a specific stakeholder requirement (e.g., show data labels, sort descending by value, and change the bar colour for negative values)?
a: Select the visual and open the Format pane (paint-brush icon). For data labels: expand 'Data labels' and toggle On; set label position (Inside end, Outside end), format (value, percentage, or custom), and font size. For sort order: click the More options (…) on the visual and select 'Sort descending' > choose the value field. Alternatively, right-click the visual header sort icon. For conditional colouring of negative bars: in Format pane > Data colors, click the fx (conditional formatting) button next to the default colour. Set 'Format by' to Rules, add a rule where the value < 0 maps to red and >= 0 maps to the default colour. You can also use field value conditional formatting to drive colours from a DAX measure that returns a hex colour string. Other key format options include: title, legend position, axis labels (X and Y), gridlines, and reference lines. Best practice: use the Format pane systematically top-to-bottom rather than ad hoc to ensure all relevant sections are reviewed.
fj: You must write a DAX measure to sort a bar chart in descending order by value.
fs: Sort order is controlled directly in the visual's More options (…) menu or the column header—no DAX is needed for standard sort by a measure or column. DAX is only needed when you want a custom sort order based on a field that differs from the display field (e.g., sorting Month Name by Month Number).

q: What is conditional formatting in Power BI, and how do you configure it on a table visual to colour-code revenue cells using a gradient scale?
a: Conditional formatting applies rules-driven formatting (background colour, font colour, data bars, or icons) to table and matrix cells based on value thresholds, field values, or rules. To apply a gradient background colour to a Revenue column: select the table visual, go to Format pane > Cell elements, toggle Background color to On for the Revenue column, then click the fx (conditional formatting) icon. In the dialog, set 'Format style' to Gradient. Define minimum, center, and maximum values (or use automatic based on field min/max) and their corresponding colours (e.g., red → white → green). Click OK. The gradient now renders proportionally across all Revenue values in the table. You can also apply conditional formatting to: font colour (same dialog, font colour option), data bars (displays a proportional bar inside the cell), and icons (maps value ranges to icons like arrows or traffic lights). Conditional formatting can be driven by a DAX measure that returns a hex colour string for fully dynamic colouring logic.
fj: Conditional formatting in Power BI requires writing a DAX measure that returns colour codes for every row.
fs: Conditional formatting has a built-in gradient and rules dialog that requires no DAX for common patterns like min-to-max colour gradients. A DAX measure returning hex colours is an optional advanced technique for fully custom logic, but it is not required for standard gradient or rule-based colouring.

q: What report-page settings can you configure in Power BI, and how do page-level and report-level filters differ in their scope?
a: Report page settings are found in Format > Page information (name, tooltip, type: regular/tooltip/drill through) and Format > Canvas settings (page size: 16:9, A4, custom pixels; scaling: fit to page, fill, actual size). In View ribbon: Page view options and Gridlines/Snap to grid. Filters operate at three scopes in the Filters pane: Report-level filters apply to all pages and all visuals in the report; Page-level filters apply only to the currently selected page and all its visuals; Visual-level filters apply only to the specific selected visual. Additionally, a fourth scope—Drillthrough filters—applies when a user arrives on a page via drillthrough. Slicers are visual-level by default but can be synced across pages using View > Sync Slicers, effectively making them act as cross-page page-level filters. The exam tests whether candidates know that report-level filters persist across page navigation, while page-level filters are isolated.
fj: Page-level filters automatically apply to all pages in the report unless you explicitly turn them off.
fs: Page-level filters are scoped to the single page on which they are configured. They do not propagate to other pages. Report-level filters are the scope that applies across all pages. This distinction is tested in scenarios where a filter is set on one page but does not appear to affect another.

q: How do visual calculations in Power BI differ from standard DAX measures, and how do you write a running total using RUNNINGSUM?
a: Visual calculations are DAX expressions defined directly on a visual, evaluated against the visual's aggregated result set rather than the underlying row-level data. This means they operate on what the visual already shows—after grouping and aggregating—making patterns like running totals, moving averages, and rank trivially simple compared to equivalent measure logic. Standard DAX measures evaluate against the model's row and filter context and have no concept of 'the row above this one in the visual'. Visual calculations were released to General Availability in May 2026. Key functions exclusive to visual calculations: RUNNINGSUM, MOVINGAVERAGE, PERCENTOFPARENT, INDEX (row reference by position), OFFSET (relative row offset), COLLAPSE/EXPAND. RUNNINGSUM([SalesAmount]) computes the cumulative sum down the visual's rows. To add a visual calculation: select a visual with an existing measure, go to Modeling > New visual calculation and type the expression. Visual calculations appear only within their visual's context and cannot be referenced by other measures or visuals.
fj: Visual calculations replace regular measures and can be referenced across all visuals on the page.
fs: Visual calculations are scoped to a single visual—they cannot be referenced by other visuals or measures on the page. They complement, rather than replace, regular measures. Standard measures remain necessary for KPI cards, cross-visual consistency, and model-wide reuse.

code.label: DAX: Visual calculation – running total
lines: -- Added as a visual calculation on a bar chart

q: How do you create a custom tooltip page in Power BI and configure a chart to display it on hover?
a: Custom tooltip pages show a full mini-report surface on hover rather than the default single-value tooltip. To create one: add a new page, go to Format > Page information and set Type to Tooltip. In Canvas settings, set the size to Tooltip (320 x 240 px by default, or a custom size matching your hover target). Design the page with visuals as normal—they will render inside the tooltip. To wire it: select the visual that should show the custom tooltip, go to Format > Tooltips, change Type from Default to Report page, and select the tooltip page from the Page dropdown. The tooltip page visuals inherit the hover context—if you hover over a specific month in a line chart, the tooltip page automatically filters to that month. Tooltip pages can contain any visual type, allowing rich context: sparklines, tables, images, or KPI cards. The tooltip page does not appear in the Pages panel during normal report navigation; it only surfaces on hover.
fj: Custom tooltip pages are configured through the Fields pane by dragging a measure into the Tooltips field well.
fs: Dragging a field into the Tooltips well in the Fields/Values section adds that field's value to the default tooltip display—it does not enable a report-page tooltip. Report-page tooltips require a separately designed tooltip page and the Tooltips > Type setting set to 'Report page' in the visual's Format pane.

q: What options exist for report navigation in Power BI, and how do you implement a page-navigator button bar?
a: Power BI supports several navigation mechanisms: (1) Page tabs at the bottom (default, can be hidden); (2) Bookmark navigator—Insert > Buttons > Navigator > Bookmark navigator, which renders buttons for each bookmark in order; (3) Page navigator—Insert > Buttons > Navigator > Page navigator, which renders buttons for each report page and automatically updates when pages are added or removed; (4) Custom buttons with Action type Bookmark or Page navigation; (5) Slicers used as tab-style selectors paired with bookmark actions. The Page Navigator is the easiest for a tab-bar UX: it creates a grouped button row where each button label matches the page name. It adapts automatically as pages are added or renamed. Style it in Format > Navigator buttons to apply consistent fill, border, and font formatting across all navigation buttons at once. For controlled navigation (e.g., a wizard flow), custom buttons with explicit Bookmark actions give more control than the auto-adapting Page Navigator.
fj: You can only navigate between report pages using the page tabs at the bottom of the canvas.
fs: Page tabs are the default navigation but are only one of multiple options. Power BI supports Page Navigators, Bookmark Navigators, and custom Action buttons for in-canvas navigation. Many production reports hide the page tabs entirely and use custom in-canvas navigation for a cleaner UX.

q: How do you design a Power BI report for mobile devices, and what is the Mobile Layout view?
a: Power BI supports a dedicated mobile canvas per report page. In the View ribbon, click Mobile layout to switch to the mobile design surface (portrait orientation, ~480 x 720 dp). A 'Visualizations' panel on the right lists all visuals from the desktop layout. Drag visuals onto the mobile canvas and resize them to fit the portrait grid. Only visuals explicitly placed on the mobile canvas appear in the Power BI mobile app—other visuals are hidden. Use the phone layout to prioritise the top 3–5 KPIs and remove or deprioritise dense tables that are unreadable at phone scale. Consider using a single-column card layout rather than side-by-side bar charts. Best practice: use large, touch-friendly slicers; increase font sizes; use high-contrast colours for readability outdoors. After saving, publish the report—the mobile layout is included automatically and surfaces when the report is viewed in the Power BI iOS or Android app in portrait mode.
fj: The mobile layout is a separate report file you publish to a mobile-specific workspace.
fs: The mobile layout is embedded within the same .pbix report file, not a separate file or workspace. It is configured in Power BI Desktop's Mobile layout view and is automatically applied when the report is viewed on a mobile device through the Power BI app.

q: What are the key accessibility requirements for Power BI reports, and how do you configure Alt Text and tab order?
a: Power BI accessibility features target keyboard-only navigation and screen-reader compatibility. Key configurations: (1) Alt text—select each visual, go to Format > General > Alt text. Enter a descriptive text that explains the visual's insight, not just its type (e.g., 'Bar chart showing Q3 revenue by region, highest in West at $4.2M'). Use the fx option to drive alt text from a DAX measure for dynamic descriptions. (2) Tab order—View > Tab order shows a numbered list of all visuals on the page. Reorder them to reflect logical reading flow (top-left to bottom-right). Set decorative shapes/images to None so screen readers skip them. (3) Colour contrast—use sufficient contrast ratios (at least 4.5:1 for text per WCAG 2.1 AA). Avoid using colour as the only differentiator—add data labels or icons. (4) Focus mode—users can expand any visual to full screen using the Focus icon; ensure visuals make sense in isolation. (5) Keyboard navigation—all slicers and filters must be operable by Tab and Enter keys without a mouse.
fj: Alt text for Power BI visuals must be a static text string entered manually for every publish cycle.
fs: Alt text can be driven by a DAX measure using the fx option in the Alt text field, allowing it to update dynamically as data changes. Static manual entry is the default but is not the only option. Dynamic alt text that describes the current data values is more accessible than a fixed description.

q: How does Power BI's anomaly detection feature work on a line chart, and what configuration options control its sensitivity?
a: Anomaly detection is an AI feature available on line charts. To enable it: select the line chart, go to the Analytics pane (the magnifying-glass icon), and expand Anomalies > Add. Power BI evaluates the time series and uses an ML algorithm to identify data points that deviate significantly from the expected range. Anomalies are marked with shapes (circles by default) on the line. A shaded band shows the expected range. When you click an anomaly marker, Power BI opens an Anomaly explanation panel that suggests which dimensions (e.g., a specific product category or region) might explain the spike using an automated driver analysis. Configuration options: Sensitivity (1–10 slider)—lower values detect fewer, larger anomalies; higher values detect smaller deviations. Expected range colour and anomaly marker shape can also be formatted. Anomaly detection is only available for visuals with a date/time axis and a numeric measure.
fj: Anomaly detection in Power BI requires you to write a custom Python or R script to flag outlier rows.
fs: Anomaly detection is a built-in AI capability in the Analytics pane—no Python or R scripting is required. It uses Power BI's native ML engine. Python/R integration exists for custom visuals but is not needed for this specific feature.

q: What AI visuals are available in Power BI, and how does the Key Influencers visual help with root-cause analysis?
a: Power BI includes several built-in AI visuals: (1) Key Influencers—identifies which factor values most correlate with an increase or decrease in a target metric. (2) Decomposition Tree—lets users drill down into a metric across multiple dimensions interactively or via AI-selected splits (AI split chooses the dimension that explains the most variance). (3) Q&A—natural-language query interface that converts typed questions into visuals. (4) Smart Narrative—AI-generated text summaries (Copilot-powered in recent versions). (5) Anomaly Detection (in the Analytics pane on line charts). For the Key Influencers visual: drag the target metric into 'Analyze' and possible factors into 'Explain by'. The visual ranks factors by their bubble size (influence magnitude) and shows the average value of the target for each factor segment. Toggle between 'Influencers' view and 'Top Segments' to see which combinations of factor values predict the target. Useful for diagnosing customer churn, high-cost incidents, or low satisfaction scores.
fj: AI visuals in Power BI require a Premium workspace and a Fabric capacity to run.
fs: Standard AI visuals—Key Influencers, Decomposition Tree, Q&A, and Smart Narrative—are available in all Power BI Desktop and service editions without a Premium or Fabric capacity requirement. Copilot-specific features (narrative Copilot, Copilot report creation) do require Fabric/Premium capacity.

q: How do you add a reference line, error bar, and forecast to a line chart in Power BI's Analytics pane?
a: The Analytics pane (magnifying-glass icon in the Visualizations pane) provides overlay analytics for supported chart types. Reference lines: expand the desired line type (Constant Line, Min/Max Line, Average Line, Median Line, Percentile Line) and click Add. Set the value, colour, style (solid/dashed/dotted), and whether to show the data label. A Constant Line at a target value (e.g., $500K monthly target) is common in KPI tracking. Error bars: available for bar and scatter charts; set to show standard deviation, standard error, or percentage bounds around each data point—useful for visualising data uncertainty. Forecasting: expand Forecast > Add on a line chart with a date axis. Configure Number of points (periods to forecast forward), Confidence interval percentage, and seasonality. Power BI uses exponential smoothing to project the line. The forecast band and line render in a lighter shade. All analytics overlays are dynamic and update with filters. Note: forecast requires Import mode; it is not supported in DirectQuery.
fj: Forecast lines in Power BI are created by writing a custom DAX measure that extends the date table.
fs: The built-in Forecast feature in the Analytics pane generates the forecast line automatically using an ML algorithm—no DAX or date table extension is required. DAX-based forecasting is an alternative for custom models, but the Analytics pane forecast is the built-in PL-300 exam–relevant approach.

q: How do you apply a custom theme to a Power BI report, and what format does a theme file use?
a: A Power BI theme is a JSON file that defines default formatting for the entire report: colour palette, font family, font sizes, visual formatting defaults (titles, backgrounds, borders), and filter pane styling. To apply: go to View > Themes > Browse for themes and select a .json theme file, or choose a built-in theme from the gallery. To customise: View > Themes > Customize current theme opens a dialog for the core palette and text options. For full control, export the current theme (View > Themes > Save current theme) as JSON and edit it—add more theme colors, change dataColors array, set visual-specific overrides in the visualStyles section. Key JSON structure: { 'name': 'MyTheme', 'dataColors': ['#hex1', ...], 'background': '#hex', 'foreground': '#hex', 'tableAccent': '#hex', 'visualStyles': {...} }. Applying a theme does not override formatting already set explicitly on individual visuals—explicit settings take precedence. Themes are stored in the .pbix file after application.
fj: To apply a theme you must publish the report, then apply the theme in the Power BI service's Report settings.
fs: Themes are applied in Power BI Desktop before or after publish—they are part of the .pbix file, not a service-level setting. The Power BI service does not have a standalone 'apply theme' setting separate from what was set in Desktop.

q: How do you configure export settings in a Power BI report, and what export options can a tenant admin restrict?
a: Export options are configured at the tenant level by the Power BI admin in the Admin portal > Tenant settings > Export and sharing settings. Admins can enable or disable: Export to Excel (.xlsx workbook or .csv), Export to PDF, Export to PowerPoint, Analyze in Excel (live connection), Publish to web (public embedding), Subscribe to reports, and Download reports (.pbix download of the file). At the report level, the report author can set report-level export restrictions in File > Settings > Report settings by limiting 'Export data' to summarized data only (no underlying row-level data). This protects sensitive data—consumers can export chart-level aggregates but not raw transaction rows. In the service, workspace admins can also restrict export at the workspace level. The distinction the exam tests: report-level settings control granularity of export (summarized vs. underlying data); tenant-level admin settings control whether export is allowed at all.
fj: Report authors can disable all export options including PDF export using the report's Format pane.
fs: The Format pane controls visual formatting, not export permissions. Export settings are configured in Report settings (File > Settings) for data granularity, and in the Admin portal's Tenant settings for enabling/disabling export features across the organisation.

q: What steps are required to create a new Power BI workspace, and what is the difference between a classic workspace and a new workspace experience?
a: To create a workspace: in the Power BI service, click Workspaces in the left navigation > Create a workspace. Provide a name, optional description, and choose the licence mode (Pro, Premium Per User, or Fabric capacity). The new workspace experience (available since 2019 and now the default) is backed by a Microsoft 365 group but decoupled from it—membership is managed directly in Power BI rather than via the M365 group. The classic workspace experience (deprecated) automatically synced membership with an M365/Office 365 group and was limited to four roles. The new workspace supports four granular roles (Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer), custom contact lists, workspace-level settings, and can be tied to a Fabric capacity or Premium Per User licence. When creating a workspace for a team's production reports, assign the workspace to a Fabric F64+ or Premium P1+ capacity for features like scheduled refresh up to 48 times per day, large semantic models, deployment pipelines, and XMLA endpoint access.
fj: To create a workspace, you must first create a Microsoft 365 Group and then Power BI generates the workspace from it.
fs: In the new workspace experience, Power BI workspaces are independent of Microsoft 365 Groups—you create them directly in the Power BI service without requiring an M365 Group. The classic workspace experience required an M365 Group, but classic workspaces are deprecated and the new experience is the current standard.

q: What is the difference between Promoted and Certified endorsement in Power BI, and who can apply each?
a: Power BI endorsement (also called content endorsement in Fabric) has two tiers. Promoted: any content owner or workspace Member/Contributor with write permissions on the content can apply the Promoted badge. It signals that the content is ready for broader use and appears with a Promoted label in the workspace and data hub. It does not require admin approval. Certified: only users designated as authorized certifiers by the Power BI tenant admin (in Admin portal > Tenant settings > Certification settings) can apply the Certified badge. Certification means the content meets organisational quality standards—validated data, tested measures, approved by a data governance team. Certified content ranks higher in search results, receives a distinct blue ribbon badge, and can be made discoverable in the OneLake data hub for users who don't yet have access. The exam tests the distinction: Promoted = self-service endorsement by the owner; Certified = admin-delegated quality gatekeeping.
fj: Certification is applied automatically by Power BI when a semantic model passes its built-in quality checks.
fs: Power BI does not have automated quality checks that apply Certified status. Certification is a human governance process where designated reviewers manually review content and apply the badge. It is a trust signal, not an automated validation.

q: When is an on-premises data gateway required in Power BI, and what is the difference between the on-premises data gateway and the personal mode gateway?
a: A gateway is required whenever the Power BI service needs to access a data source that is not reachable from the public internet—primarily on-premises SQL Server, Oracle, SAP, file shares, or any cloud database behind a private network/VPN. For Import mode semantic models, the gateway brokers the scheduled refresh: Power BI service sends a refresh request, the gateway queries the on-premises source, and sends data back encrypted. For DirectQuery and Live Connection, every single user query is proxied through the gateway in real time. The on-premises data gateway (standard mode) is installed on a server, supports multiple concurrent connections, supports all connector types including DirectQuery, live connection, and composite models, and is shared across multiple users and semantic models. The personal mode gateway is installed on a personal machine, supports only a single user, supports only Import-mode scheduled refresh (no DirectQuery or live connection), and is not suitable for production shared reports. Use standard gateway for production; personal gateway only for individual developer testing.
fj: You need a gateway for all Power BI data sources, including data stored in Azure SQL Database.
fs: Azure cloud services that are publicly accessible (Azure SQL Database, Azure Blob Storage, Snowflake, etc.) do not require a gateway—Power BI service can connect directly. A gateway is only required for on-premises sources or cloud sources behind a private endpoint/VPN that is not reachable from the Power BI service's network.

q: How do you configure a scheduled refresh for a semantic model in the Power BI service, and what are the refresh frequency limits?
a: After publishing a report, navigate to the semantic model in the Power BI service > Settings > Scheduled refresh. Toggle the slider to On, select the refresh frequency (Daily or Weekly), choose time slots (up to 8 times per day on Shared/Pro capacity), and set the time zone. If the semantic model uses an on-premises data source, the gateway connection must be configured first in Settings > Gateway connection. On Premium capacity (P1+) or Fabric (F64+) the limit increases to 48 times per day (every 30 minutes). Incremental refresh can further reduce refresh time by only processing new/changed data partitions rather than reloading the full table. The semantic model owner's credentials are used by default for refresh; you can change the 'Data source credentials' to a service account for unattended refresh. Failed refreshes generate email notifications to the semantic model owner—configure this in Settings > Scheduled refresh > Send refresh failure notifications.
fj: Shared capacity workspaces allow semantic models to refresh up to 48 times per day.
fs: The 48-times-per-day limit applies to Premium capacity (P1+) and Fabric capacity (F64+). Shared capacity workspaces are limited to 8 refreshes per day. This difference is a frequently tested PL-300 exam detail.

q: What are the four workspace roles in Power BI, and which actions can each role perform?
a: Power BI workspaces have four roles with progressively broader permissions. Viewer: can view reports, dashboards, and data; cannot edit, publish, or delete content; RLS rules apply to Viewers. Contributor: can create, edit, and delete their own content in the workspace; can publish reports; cannot manage workspace settings, add members, or update apps. Member: all Contributor permissions plus the ability to publish and update the workspace app, allow others to reshare content, feature dashboards, and add other Members or Contributors (but not Admins). Admin: full control—all Member permissions plus ability to add/remove any role including other Admins, delete the workspace, update workspace settings, and manage gateway connections. Key RLS note: workspace Admins, Members, and Contributors see all data regardless of RLS rules because they have edit permissions on the semantic model. Only Viewers are subject to RLS. If you want RLS enforced for a team member, assign them the Viewer role, not Contributor.
fj: Contributors can add new members to a workspace.
fs: Contributors can manage their own content but cannot manage workspace membership. Adding users to the workspace is a Member-level permission (for adding Members and Contributors) or Admin-level (for adding Admins). This distinction matters when the exam tests which role is sufficient for a described scenario.

q: Walk through how to configure static row-level security (RLS) in Power BI Desktop and then assign users to a role in the Power BI service.
a: In Power BI Desktop, go to Modeling > Manage Roles > Create. Name the role (e.g., 'West Region'), select the table to filter (e.g., DimSalesTerritory), and enter a DAX filter expression: [Region] = "West". Click Save. This static RLS hardcodes the region filter into the role. For dynamic RLS, use USERPRINCIPALNAME() or USERNAME() to match the logged-in user to a table: [SalesRepEmail] = USERPRINCIPALNAME(). Publish the report to the Power BI service. In the service, navigate to the semantic model > More options > Security. On the Row-Level Security page, click the role name to expand it and add users by email address or Active Directory security groups. Click the '...' next to a role and Test as role to verify the filter is applied correctly. Important: workspace Admins, Members, and Contributors bypass RLS because they have model edit permissions. Assign users who must have RLS enforced the Viewer workspace role, or share the report/app with them without workspace membership.
fj: RLS filters apply to workspace Admins and Members as long as they are added to an RLS role in the service.
fs: RLS is bypassed for users with Admin, Member, or Contributor workspace roles regardless of whether they are added to an RLS role. The Power BI service grants these roles implicit full-data access because they have model edit rights. Only Viewer-role users (or users who access content via an app or shared link without workspace membership) are subject to RLS.

code.label: DAX: Dynamic RLS filter using USERPRINCIPALNAME
lines: -- In Manage Roles, on the SalesRep table

q: What are sensitivity labels in Power BI, how are they applied, and what behaviour do they trigger when data is exported?
a: Sensitivity labels are classifications from Microsoft Purview Information Protection (formerly Azure Information Protection) applied to Power BI content—semantic models, reports, dashboards, and dataflows—to indicate data sensitivity (e.g., Public, General, Confidential, Highly Confidential). To apply a label in the Power BI service: open the item's Settings (or More options > Settings), expand the Sensitivity label section, and select the appropriate label. Labels can also be applied in bulk or enforced via tenant-level mandatory labelling policies. Within the Power BI service, the label affects discoverability and auditing but does not restrict access—access is governed by workspace roles and RLS. The label's protection is enforced when data leaves the service: when a user exports to Excel, PDF, or PowerPoint, or downloads the .pbix file, the Purview-defined encryption and access policies associated with the label are applied to the exported file. This means a Highly Confidential export may be encrypted so that only authorised domain users can open it. Prerequisites: Microsoft 365 E3 or E5 licence, or Azure Information Protection P1/P2; a Purview sensitivity label policy published to Power BI users.
fj: Sensitivity labels encrypt all data within the Power BI semantic model, preventing unauthorised users from running queries.
fs: Sensitivity labels do not encrypt data inside the Power BI service or restrict query access. Data access is controlled by workspace roles and RLS. Label-based encryption is applied to exported files only (Excel, PDF, .pbix), not to in-service queries.

q: How do you configure and update a Power BI app, and what is the difference between sharing a workspace versus publishing an app?
a: A Power BI app is a curated, read-only package of reports and dashboards published from a workspace, with its own permission list independent of workspace roles. To create or update an app: in the workspace, click Create app (or Update app if one exists). Configure: name, description, logo, app navigation (which reports/dashboards to include and in what order, with section headings and links), access permissions (specific users/groups or the whole organisation), and contact details. Apps are the recommended distribution method for broad audiences—consumers get a clean, navigable collection of content without seeing the workspace's full editing interface. Sharing a workspace grants workspace roles (which may allow editing); sharing an app grants read-only access to the published content bundle. App permissions are separate from workspace permissions: someone can have workspace Viewer access and app access simultaneously, or just app access with no workspace visibility. When you update the app after publishing new reports, the update is immediate for all app consumers without requiring them to re-install.
fj: Updating a report in the workspace automatically updates the app for all users.
fs: Publishing a new report to the workspace does not automatically include it in or update the app. You must explicitly update the app (Update app button in the workspace) to push changes to app consumers. This separation allows a workspace to be a staging area while the app remains on a stable version until explicitly updated.

q: What are the supported methods for publishing or importing content to a Power BI workspace, and when would you use each?
a: There are four main ways to get content into a workspace. (1) Publish from Power BI Desktop: File > Publish > Select workspace. This is the most common method for .pbix files—it uploads the report and semantic model together. (2) Import from the Power BI service: in the workspace, click New > Import > select a local .pbix, Excel, or CSV file. Excel workbooks can be imported as data or as a live Excel-like view. (3) Deployment pipelines: for Dev > Test > Prod promotion. Configure a pipeline in the workspace, assign stages, and use Deploy to push content between stages without re-publishing from Desktop. (4) Power BI REST API / fabric CLI: programmatic publish for CI/CD automation. Also, OneLake shortcuts and Direct Lake semantic models (Fabric) create connections to lake data without uploading files. The Update app action after any of the above pushes the new content version to app consumers. The exam may test that publishing from Desktop overwrites the existing semantic model if the name matches, while Import creates a new item.
fj: Deployment pipelines are only available in Fabric workspaces tied to a Fabric capacity.
fs: Deployment pipelines require a Premium capacity (P1+) or Fabric capacity—not exclusively Fabric. They are available on any workspace assigned to a Premium Per User, Premium capacity, or Fabric capacity licence. Shared capacity workspaces do not support deployment pipelines.

q: What is a Power BI dashboard, and how does it differ from a report? How do you create a dashboard?
a: A Power BI dashboard is a single-page canvas in the Power BI service containing tiles—pinned visuals from reports, live report pages, streaming data widgets, images, or web content. Unlike reports (multi-page, interactive, with filters and slicers on the canvas), dashboards are read-only summary views; clicking a tile navigates back to the underlying report. Dashboards cannot be created in Power BI Desktop—they exist only in the service. To create a dashboard: open a report in the service, hover over a visual, and click the pin icon to pin it to an existing or new dashboard. Alternatively, use 'Pin live page' to pin an entire report page as a single tile that remains interactive. Dashboards support data alerts (thresholds that trigger email/mobile notifications when a KPI tile crosses a value) and can subscribe viewers to automatic email snapshots. Key distinction for the exam: reports are the authoring layer (Desktop + service); dashboards are the operational monitoring layer (service only).
fj: Dashboards can be created in Power BI Desktop and then published to the service.
fs: Dashboards are a Power BI service–only feature. Power BI Desktop creates reports (.pbix files). Dashboards are assembled in the service by pinning report visuals or live pages. You cannot create or view a dashboard in Desktop.

q: What distribution methods are available for sharing Power BI content, and how do you choose the right one?
a: Power BI offers several distribution options: (1) Workspace sharing—grants a workspace role to individuals or groups; best for team collaboration where members need to edit or manage content. (2) Report/dashboard direct sharing—shares a specific item to named users; simplest for ad hoc access; requires the recipient to have a Pro licence (or the workspace to be on Premium/Fabric). (3) App publishing—a curated bundle of content with its own navigation and access list; best for broad, stable distribution to business consumers. (4) Embed in SharePoint Online or Teams—embeds a live report using the Power BI web part; consumers need a Pro licence or the workspace must be on Premium/Fabric. (5) Publish to web—creates a public anonymous URL; insecure, not for internal data. (6) Paginated reports subscription—delivers scheduled PDF/Excel exports by email. Choose: Workspace role for editors; App for large consumer audiences; Direct share for individual one-off access; SharePoint/Teams embed for portal integration; Publish to web only for truly public datasets.
fj: Publish to web is suitable for sharing internal financial reports with employees who have Power BI Pro licences.
fs: Publish to web generates a public, anonymous URL accessible to anyone on the internet—it bypasses all authentication and RLS. It must never be used for internal or sensitive data. Internal sharing should use workspace roles, direct share, or app distribution, all of which enforce authentication and permissions.

q: How do you set up a data alert and a report subscription in the Power BI service, and what are their respective use cases?
a: Data alerts monitor KPI tile values on dashboards and notify the owner (and optionally others) when a value crosses a threshold. To create one: on a dashboard, click the More options (…) on a KPI, gauge, or card tile > Manage alerts > Add alert rule. Set the condition (Above/Below), threshold value, and maximum notification frequency (at most once per day, or once per hour). The alert triggers only for the tile owner—you cannot set alerts on behalf of other users. Alerts are evaluated when the underlying semantic model refreshes. Report subscriptions send a snapshot of a report page (as an email image or PDF attachment) to a list of recipients on a schedule. To create a subscription: open the report > Subscribe (envelope icon) > Create email subscription. Configure the schedule (daily, weekly, after refresh), time, recipient list (Pro licence required per recipient), and whether to include a link. Use data alerts for real-time threshold monitoring (e.g., alert when inventory drops below 100); use subscriptions for recurring scheduled delivery of report snapshots to stakeholders who prefer email over actively logging in.
fj: Data alerts can be configured by any report viewer with a Viewer workspace role.
fs: Data alerts can only be set on dashboard tiles by the tile owner. Viewers can receive subscription emails but cannot configure alerts on dashboards they do not own. Dashboard alert configuration requires at least Contributor access to pin tiles and own a dashboard.

q: What is item-level access in Power BI, and how does it differ from workspace role assignment?
a: Item-level access allows you to share a specific report, dashboard, or semantic model with individual users or groups without granting them a workspace role. This means the recipient can access only that single item—they do not see other workspace content, cannot navigate the workspace, and cannot edit anything. To share: in the workspace, click More options (…) on the item > Share, enter email addresses, choose whether to allow resharing and whether to grant Build permission on the underlying semantic model. Item-level access is appropriate for: one-off report distribution to external stakeholders, granting a single team read access to one report while others in the workspace remain restricted, and giving service accounts or embedded scenarios access to specific semantic models. Workspace role assignment grants access to all content in the workspace and is appropriate for teams who work collaboratively on all workspace items. The key exam distinction: item-level = granular, item-specific; workspace role = broad, workspace-wide.
fj: Item-level sharing automatically grants the recipient a Viewer workspace role so they can navigate the workspace.
fs: Item-level sharing does not assign any workspace role. Recipients can only access the shared item—they do not see the workspace or other content within it. This is by design for controlled distribution without exposing the full workspace.

q: How do you control access to a shared semantic model in Power BI, and what does 'Build permission' allow a consumer to do?
a: Semantic model access is managed separately from report sharing. A shared semantic model allows multiple report authors to connect from Power BI Desktop or the service without each building their own copy of the model. In the Power BI service, navigate to the semantic model > More options > Manage permissions. Grant users Read permission (can query the model via reports) or Build permission (can create new reports, export underlying data, and use Analyze in Excel against the model). Build permission is required when a consumer wants to: create a new report on top of the shared semantic model from Power BI Desktop (File > Get Data > Power BI semantic models); use Analyze in Excel to pivot against the model; use composite models that layer on top of the shared model. Without Build permission, the user can only view reports already created on the semantic model. For governance: keep Build permission restricted to trusted report authors; give business consumers Read-only access via app or item-level sharing.
fj: Read permission on a semantic model allows users to create new reports against it in Power BI Desktop.
fs: Read permission only allows consumption of the semantic model through existing reports. Creating new reports against a semantic model from Desktop or the service requires Build permission. This distinction is specifically tested in PL-300 scenarios about semantic model governance.
